title: Fitting tongue-and-groove MDF wall panelling
subtitle: Panel a wall from floor to dado height using pre-primed MDF boards
excerpt: How to fit pre-primed tongue-and-groove MDF wall panelling to a dado height, covering marking the dado line, fixing a top rail and bottom rail, nailing the boards through the tongue, filling nail holes, and priming for paint.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (wall panelling, wainscot). Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery.

glossaryTerms.definition: A joinery profile where one edge of a board has a projecting tongue and the opposite edge has a matching groove. The tongue of each board slots into the groove of its neighbour, giving an interlocked surface. When used for wall panelling, nails or staples are driven through the tongue at an angle, hiding the fixings in the joint.

The lower portion of a wall, typically from the floor to approximately 900 mm height, where a dado rail traditionally marks the boundary between the lower panelled zone and the upper plastered or papered zone. Named after the die of a classical column pedestal.

Wall panelling made from medium-density fibreboard. Pre-primed MDF tongue-and-groove boards are the standard modern substitute for solid pine panelling. MDF is dimensionally stable, paints to a smooth finish, and costs significantly less than solid timber. It must not be used in areas subject to permanent moisture.

content.text: Tongue-and-groove
MDF panelling
fitted to
dado height
is fixed to the wall with a combination of adhesive and nails through the tongue. The tongue carries the fixing, which is hidden in the
tongue-and-groove

joint. A top rail (the dado rail) and a bottom rail (a skirting or base trim) frame the boards and carry the horizontal load. The job suits an intermediate carpenter who is comfortable with a hand saw and a spirit level.
What you will need: materials

Pre-primed MDF tongue-and-groove boards (typically 95 mm face width, 9 mm thick, 2400 mm long). A top rail (dado rail or a 45 x 20 mm MDF strip). A bottom rail (a narrow skirting or a 45 x 20 mm MDF strip to sit on top of the existing skirting). Panel adhesive or construction adhesive in a cartridge. Oval wire nails, 40 mm, for the tongue fixings. One-coat filler for any filled nail heads at the edges. Interior wood primer and paint.
Step 1, check the wall before you cut

Sweep a cable-and-pipe detector across the wall at the dado height and at the floor level. Mark any hits with pencil. At dado height, cables commonly run from light switches horizontally to the consumer unit; at floor level, they run along the skirting to sockets. Any cable or pipe in the area must be mapped before you nail through the wall. Keep fixings away from any marked zone by at least 50 mm.
Step 2, mark the dado height and fix the rails

Mark the dado line at 900 mm height (or the height that matches existing dado rails in the room) using a pencil and a long spirit level. Fix the top rail along this line with No. 8 x 50 mm screws into wall plugs at 400 mm centres. Fix the bottom rail along the top of the existing skirting or along the floor level. Check both rails are level and parallel: the panelling boards will be vertical only if the rails are correct. On a masonry wall, use the SDS drill with a 6 mm masonry bit.
Step 3, fit the first board

Start from an internal corner. Cut the first board to length: the height between the bottom of the top rail and the top of the bottom rail, less 2 mm for clearance. Spread a bead of panel adhesive in a zigzag on the back face, and press the board to the wall with the grooved edge tight into the corner. Check it is plumb with the spirit level in both directions. Hold it with masking tape or clamp it to the rail while the adhesive grips. Drive a 40 mm oval nail through the tongue at an angle of about 45 degrees, at top and bottom, where it will be hidden when the next board's groove slides over.
Step 4, fit subsequent boards

Slide the groove of each subsequent board over the tongue of the last. Apply adhesive to the board back, press it to the wall, and nail through the tongue. Continue across the wall. At external corners or doorway edges, the last board will need to be face-nailed (nails through the board face rather than the tongue); fill and sand these nail heads before priming. Check plumb every three or four boards: any drift accumulates and the final board at an external corner will be noticeably out.
Step 5, fit internal corner boards and fill

At an internal corner, the final board must be scribed to the wall: hold it in position and run a pencil along the wall face, then cut along the pencil line with a coping saw or jigsaw. This compensates for any wall irregularity. Fill any exposed nail heads with one-coat filler, sand flush, then apply an interior primer to the raw MDF edges and any unprimed areas before painting the full surface.
Variations

Full-height panelling from floor to ceiling follows the same method. Add a skirting at the base and a cornice or picture rail at the top. For a feature wall, a shaker-panel effect is made by gluing narrow MDF strips over the tongue-and-groove in a grid. Fill the joints, then paint the whole surface one colour.
Care

MDF panelling decorated with a quality interior emulsion or eggshell requires no special maintenance. Wipe with a damp cloth. Repaint every five to eight years or when the surface starts to look tired. Keep the area above the skirting dry: MDF absorbs water at cut edges and will swell if repeatedly wet.

attrs.symptom: Boards not sitting flat against the wall; a hollow behind the board in the middle
attrs.cause: The wall is bowed; the boards bridge the hollow rather than following it.
attrs.fix: Apply more adhesive to the back of the board in the hollow area and press it home. If the wall bow is more than 5 mm, pack the hollow with a thin strip of MDF fixed to the wall first so the board has a flat surface to adhere to.

attrs.symptom: Gaps appearing between boards after a few weeks
attrs.cause: MDF expanding or contracting with humidity changes; boards not acclimatised before fitting.
attrs.fix: MDF is more stable than solid timber but still moves. Leave boards flat in the room for 24 hours before fitting. A small gap is normal; fill with a flexible decorator's caulk that stays flexible under movement.

attrs.symptom: The top rail is not level across the room
attrs.cause: Marked from one point without checking at both ends; the ceiling is not parallel to the floor.
attrs.fix: Use the spirit level to re-mark the top rail line at multiple points across the room. The rail must be level even if it means an unequal gap at the ceiling. Cover the gap with a cornice or coving strip.
